# Runbook — On-disk log-directory rotation (watchdog Check W) (Successor-Remediation, Op Stds §43)

<!-- BEGIN TREE-XREF (generated by scripts/build_runbook_xrefs.py — do not edit inside) -->
> **In the troubleshooting tree** ([printable guide](../troubleshooting/troubleshooting_guide.md) · dashboard `/troubleshoot`). This runbook resolves:
>   - The daemon plane — liveness, breaker, alerts, row-cap, picklists, guards → On-disk log-directory rotation (archive, never delete) → “The watchdog reports the on-disk log directory is growing / rotation is straining, or disk is under pressure.”
> Daemon(s): `watchdog` — see the [daemon reference](../references/daemon_reference.md).
<!-- END TREE-XREF -->

A §43 successor-remediation entry, written for the **Successor-Operator**: a
trained operator who runs Claude Code and reads Smartsheet rows + alert emails,
but does **not** read code or touch secrets. The §42 code-reader rationale lives
in `shared/log_rotation.py` and `scripts/watchdog.py::_check_log_dir_rotation`.

## Purpose

What to do when the daily watchdog reports on the **on-disk `~/its/logs` directory**
(error_code `log_dir_rotation`). Check W is the growth **bound** for the log
directory — the sibling of Check O (`row_cap_rotation`, which bounds the Smartsheet
`ITS_Errors` / `ITS_Review_Queue` sheets). Where Check O prunes sheet rows, Check W
keeps the on-disk log directory from growing without limit.

**v1 ARCHIVES, it does NOT delete.** Two archive actions run on every watchdog cycle,
both non-destructive:

- **Daily files** (`logs/<YYYY-MM-DD>.log`) older than 14 days are **gzipped in place**
  to a sibling `<name>.gz` — the original is compressed, never unlinked (~12–13× smaller;
  zero forensic loss, the content is fully recoverable with `gunzip`).
- **launchd `.out.log` files** are **copied → gzipped → truncated in place** (the file is
  never renamed or unlinked — launchd holds the open fd, so only `truncate` is safe; a
  rename or delete would strand the daemon writing to a zero-byte or orphaned inode).

There is **no delete stage in v1** — nothing this check does is irreversible, so there is
never anything destructive to undo.

## Procedure

### Symptom

- A **watchdog record** (WARN or CRITICAL) with `error_code = log_dir_rotation`,
  `Script = scripts.watchdog`. A **WARN** means rotation ran but the directory is still
  large / straining (e.g. approaching a disk-space threshold after a clean archive pass).
  A **CRITICAL** means the rotation pass itself could not make progress — a wedged pruner,
  an unreadable/unwritable `logs/` directory, or disk genuinely full.
- In **ITS_Errors**: a `Script = scripts.watchdog` row whose message names the `logs/`
  directory size / the archive result.
- Observable on the dashboard watchdog panel as the Check W line.

### What the Successor-Operator checks

1. **Did the rotation pass run this cycle, and what did it archive?** Read the watchdog
   record / dashboard line — it reports how many daily files were gzipped and the launchd
   `.out.log` truncation result. A clean run that still WARNs points at **disk capacity**,
   not the pruner.
2. **Free disk + the logs directory.** Ask Claude to check free disk space and the size of
   `~/its/logs`. A `logs/` directory that is large but *shrinking* run-over-run is healthy;
   one that keeps growing after a clean archive pass is the real problem.
3. **Is it a one-off transient?** A single WARN that clears on the next daily watchdog run
   (the next `logs/` file rolls over, the archive catches up) is self-healing — no action.

### The Claude prompt or UI action

There is **nothing destructive to undo** (v1 never deletes). The low-class repair is to
re-run the pass and confirm it archives:

> "Claude, the watchdog reported `log_dir_rotation` (Check W). Please re-run the watchdog
> log-rotation pass in **--dry** preview first so I can see what it *would* archive, then a
> real run; report how many daily files were gzipped and whether the launchd .out.log files
> were truncated. Also show me free disk and the current size of ~/its/logs."

If `--dry` shows the expected daily-file gzips + launchd truncations and a real run brings
the directory down, the WARN was a lag and it is resolved. Because every action is an
archive (gzip / copy-truncate), a re-run is always safe to repeat.

### Escalate-to-Seth condition

Escalate to the **Developer-Operator (Seth)** when:

- The check is **persistently CRITICAL** across watchdog runs, or the rotation pass is
  **wedged** (it runs but archives nothing while `logs/` keeps growing) — that is a
  **code-class** fault in `shared/log_rotation.py` (a fixed high-capability-class category).
- **Disk keeps filling** after a clean archive run — a **capacity-class** problem; the
  archive bound alone cannot free space that compression no longer recovers.
- **Anything about the off-host / backup copy of the archives** — deciding whether, where,
  or how the `.gz` archives are copied off the host (and any eventual delete stage) is a
  **doctrine / retention decision that is Seth's**, never actioned unsupervised. v1
  deliberately ships archive-only; the irreversible delete stage is a separate later change.

Both-rule (Op Stds §44): "re-run the archive pass + confirm + report" is the Tier-2 action;
a wedged pruner (code), a disk that stays full (capacity), or the off-host-copy/delete-stage
decision (doctrine) are high-class → Tier 3 (Seth).

## Owner

`@solutionsmith`. Any change to the 14-day age cutoff, the archive mechanism, or the
introduction of a delete stage is a code change (Tier 3) — the constants live in
`shared/defaults.py` (Check O precedent; zero ITS_Config rows).
